Sleep Experts
There have been many books written by experts, a few of them doctors and a
whole slew of sleep consultants. I will give you a short summary of three
methods by three doctors who all run sleep labs and have conducted many years
of research in sleep disorders.
Richard Ferber theory

y
Dr Ferber is the director of the Sleep Lab, Boston Children’s Hospital, Harvard.
According to him, what parents perceive as abnormal awakenings at night is
actually normal and what they do to treat these abnormal awakenings is
abnormal. In the first 4 months, it is normal to wake up a couple of times to feed.
It is during these times that you need to start your sleep routines, so that by the
time your child is 6 months, she is sleeping 6 hours at a stretch. If this is not the
case at 6 months, then you need to start training her. Sleep training can make
your little one sleep for 6 hours at a stretch by various methods listed here, but it
is important to stick with the method you choose to go with. Babies under 4

training her. Sleep training can make
your little one sleep for 6 hours at a stretch by various methods listed here, but it
is important to stick with the method you choose to go with. Babies under 4
months are not neurologically developed to self-soothe and go to sleep, so you
cannot use this technique for babies this age.
Ferber method
Otherwise called graduated extinction, this is a specific method of extinction
sleep training (minimal parental interference to teach babies to self-soothe and
go to sleep) or the ‘cry-it-out’ method. It really works, and that too quickly, in 3
to 5 days. The main disadvantage is, obviously, the crying. For most parents,
including me, allowing your baby to cry for even a few seconds seems cruel.

. It really works, and that too quickly, in 3
to 5 days. The main disadvantage is, obviously, the crying. For most parents,
including me, allowing your baby to cry for even a few seconds seems cruel.
There is evidence from an evolutionary standpoint that we are wired to respond
to our children crying. In most child-rearing ventures, it is important to be
consistent. This method should only take a few days, not many weeks.
After following your usual sleep routine for your baby, place your sleepy
infant in his bed when he is still awake. You then leave the room. Check in on
your child over increasing time intervals. If he starts crying, wait for a specific
amount of time. If you give in and pick up, rock or feed the baby, it will prolong

then leave the room. Check in on
your child over increasing time intervals. If he starts crying, wait for a specific
amount of time. If you give in and pick up, rock or feed the baby, it will prolong
the process. Dr Ferber suggests 3 minutes the first night before you go in.
Comforting the little one should only include patting his back or talking to
him in a soothing voice. But it should not include picking up the baby, rocking,
feeding or turning on the light. You should be out of the room in 2 minutes.
After leaving the room, the next time you wait out for 5 minutes to allow the
baby to cry before you intervene. Dr Ferber calls this progressive waiting. You
may need to go back in a couple of times, extending the waiting period to 10

minutes till he falls asleep by himself without you being in the room. If your
baby wakes up in the middle of the night, you may need to repeat this again.
The second day you will wait 5 minutes to go in initially, then 10 and
subsequently 12 minutes.
On the third day, begin with 10 minutes, then 12 and then 15 minutes. The
idea is with each passing day, by increasing the waiting-out time, your baby will
learn to fall asleep on his own without the parents’ help.
The second and third night will be the toughest for parents and this is called
extinction burst – it is around this time that most parents give up. Don’t give up
just then because it gets steadily better after this, and your baby should be
sleeping on his own soon.

Dr Jodi Mindell method
The third method is by Dr Jodi Mindell, the director of the Sleep Disorder
Centre at Children’s Hospital, Philadelphia. According to her, a quarter to a third
of all parents of babies younger than 3 years of age report that their kid suffers

from some sort of a sleep problem. She believes that as most of these are new
parents, they are wrong in assuming there is a lurking problem. This is not to say
that baby sleep problems don’t exist. It just means that baby sleep patterns are
crazy and if your baby wakes up a couple of times at night, it is perfectly normal.
Establish good sleep routines and be consistent: Even if your baby
has sleep problems, establishing a routine will go a long way in sorting
it out.
Enforcing an early bedtime every night: Babies who go to bed late
will not only take longer to fall asleep but will also wake up frequently
at night. If your baby is overtired, there are more chances that she will
wake up more often.

In this book, the authors promote that parents should build a flexible routine for babies from a

the needs of the baby and not a schedule dictated by parents. The authors’ attitude towards
sleeping is that the child should be taught to sleep in her own bed and should learn to calm

between Richard Ferber’s method and co-sleeping in parents’ bed.

Q My baby has GER. Will this delay his sleeping through the night?
Yes, unfortunately babies with GER take longer to sleep through the night.
The symptoms of heartburn get exaggerated when lying down flat and
hence it is worse at night. Your baby may be on medication for these
symptoms, so make sure the dosage is adjusted as he gets heavier.
Crying usually increases GER symptoms, so you cannot let them cry for too
long when you are trying to sleep train them. Further, research shows that
children who are tired and cranky due to lack of sleep are less interested in
learning and acquiring new knowledge. So it’s important that both children

and parents get a good night’s sleep. Usually, by 6 months, the symptoms
start to abate.
Sudden Infant Death Syndrome (SIDS)
New parents do everything they can to keep their baby healthy and safe. But
sometimes, for no apparent reason, a baby who seems perfectly healthy passes
away. In a baby less than a year, doctors refer to it as SIDS. Since it usually
happens when the baby is asleep, it is also known as cot death.
What causes SIDS?
Doctors are not sure, but sometimes it may be because of genetics. Other babies
may be born with a problem in the part of the brain that controls breathing, heart
rate, blood pressure, temperature and waking from sleep. Factors that raise the
risk incidence of SIDS are:
Brain defects
Age below 6 months
Prematurity
Poor sleep position
Second-hand smoke

g, heart
rate, blood pressure, temperature and waking from sleep. Factors that raise the
risk incidence of SIDS are:
Brain defects
Age below 6 months
Prematurity
Poor sleep position
Second-hand smoke
Upper respiratory infection
Who does it affect?
AGE: The age group that is commonly affected is 1 to 4 months,
although it can affect any baby under 1 year.
SEX: It’s more likely to affect boys.
RACE: It is seen more often in African Americans and Alaskan
natives. There is really no reason found for this predilection.
BIRTH WEIGHT: Preterm and small-for-date babies are more

susceptible.
FAMILY HISTORY: More likely to happen if a sibling, or first-
degree relative, has passed away due to SIDS.
MATERNAL HEALTH: The younger the mother, the higher the risk
– especially if she has not received good antenatal care. Other risk
factors are if she was a smoker during pregnancy, or if she consumed
excessive alcohol.
Prevention strategies
Put your baby on his back to sleep. Only once the baby can roll on his
tummy is it safe for him to sleep on his tummy. Until then, remember
the phrase ‘back to sleep’.
Choose a firm flat mattress for the cot. Use tight-fitting sheets for the
mattress. Keep stuffed toys, blankets, bolsters and pillows out of the
bed till at least 1 year of age. You can swaddle him to keep him warm
but not after he learns to roll.

If you are lucky,
you may find that by the time your baby is 3 months of age, he may go 6
hours without a feed and allow you to sleep for 6 hours.
QHow do I know that my baby is getting enough milk?
The baby will latch well and feed vigorously around 8 times a day.
You’ll feel fullness before the feed and the breast will feel empty at the end
of the feed. There will be no nipple soreness or engorgement.
The baby will be satisfied for at least 2 hours and sometimes go 3–4 hours
before asking for a feed.
If the baby is feeding well, the stools will turn from dark green to yellow,

Sleep in the same room but not in the same bed. Sharing the room with

Vaccination and breastfeeding also reduce the risk.
Keep the baby cool while he is asleep. Don’t overdress the baby when

Don’t smoke or drink or use drugs.

Breastfeeding
By the time your baby is born, you would have some idea about how you’re
going to feed her. You will receive lots of advice from family, friends and well-
wishers about what and how to feed the baby. The final decision, however, must
be made by you – the primary caregiver. Medical bodies, such as the Indian
Academy of Paediatrics (IAP), the WHO and the American Academy of
Paediatrics (AAP), strongly recommend exclusively breastfeeding for 6
months. After that, they say, one can continue breastfeeding for up to a year at
least, along with giving solid foods such as fruits, vegetables, grains and
protein sources that should be started at 6 months of age.

Over the next few pages, I will describe both the advantages and
disadvantages of breastfeeds and formula feeds. Like every other medical body,
I will try and convince you that breastmilk is the perfect food for your baby.
Although formula companies have attempted to mimic breastmilk in terms of
nutrients, their product falls short in comparison to the real thing. Before we
move ahead, let’s look at some of the commonly used terms in relation to
breastfeeding.
Common Breastfeeding Terms
Colostrum
This is the first milk that the breasts produce. It is thin and watery; some mothers

may produce it even before the baby is born. It is rich in the mother’s
antibodies and has fewer calories than mature milk because of its reduced fat
content.
Foremilk
This is the milk that comes out first when the baby starts feeding. It contains less
fat. Babies who tend to have short feeds or snack end up getting mainly this
foremilk. Hence, they feed frequently and may even be fussy because they’re
hungry.
Hindmilk
This is the milk that comes out during the latter part of the feeding. It tends to
contain more fat and babies who feed only from one breast and drain the breast
completely tend to get more of this milk, and hence, are more satisfied.
Engorgement
This happens in the first few days when you start producing the mature milk –
the breasts feel hard, sore, lumpy and full.

Engorgement
This happens in the first few days when you start producing the mature milk –
the breasts feel hard, sore, lumpy and full.
Plugged/blocked ducts
This happens when there is blockage in one of the milk ducts, creating a very
hard, tender area. It has a risk of getting infected.
Inverted nipples
It refers to an indentation at the centre of the nipple due to which babies find it
difficult to latch (see p. 83–84). You can try and pull out an inverted nipple
using a syringe – nursing staff at the hospital or midwives who conduct the

Advantages of Breastfeeding
Breast milk has the ideal ingredients for your baby’s growth.
It contains the mother’s antibodies, which will in turn protect the baby
from various infections the mother has been exposed to. It has been
observed that breastfed babies develop immunity from recurrent colds
and tummy upsets. One of the main benefits of exclusive breastfeeding
up to 6 months is protection against gastrointestinal infections. The

Babies are very rarely allergic to breast milk. Instead, it reduces food

A, D, G, M and E). Each serves its own function to help the body stay

Exclusively breastfed babies have reduced risk of asthma, diabetes

Breastfeeding makes it easier for mothers to lose their pregnancy

How to Encourage Breastfeeding
Hospitals should encourage rooming with your baby as soon as the
baby is born or checked over.
For the first 1 or 2 days of the baby’s birth, nursing staff or lactation

How to Encourage Breastfeeding
Hospitals should encourage rooming with your baby as soon as the
baby is born or checked over.
For the first 1 or 2 days of the baby’s birth, nursing staff or lactation
consultants should evaluate whether the baby is latching on and that the
breastfeeding position is right.
Water, formula or sugar water should not be offered to babies unless
prescribed by the doctor.
Pacifiers should not be used until the baby is breastfeeding well. Since
the two kinds of sucking are different, she may not learn how to
breastfeed well.
After being discharged from the hospital, babies should be seen by a
paediatrician for the first 2 weeks to assess breastfeeding.

When to Top Up
After a very long feed, if the baby still shows signs of hunger,
it usually means that you may need to give her a top-up feed.
The other situation is when even after a long feed, the baby
appears satisfied but gets hungry within an hour. You can try
breastfeeding again but, in some cases, you may need to give
a top-up.
Some mothers are very motivated and don’t want to offer
formula. But if your baby is losing weight and not passing
adequate urine, then it’s a case for doing so. I have had
instances when babies have lost huge amounts of weight

Medical Considerations with Breastfeeding
In a few situations, breast milk could cause more harm to the baby. You should
not breastfeed in the following cases:

You are HIV-positive (as the virus can pass through breast milk to the
baby).
You have an active, open case of tuberculosis of the lungs that has not
been treated.
You are receiving chemotherapy for cancer.
You are using illegal drugs like cocaine or marijuana.
Your baby has a rare condition called galactosaemia and cannot
tolerate sugar galactose that is present in breast milk.
You are taking prescription medications for conditions such as
arthritis.
Tips: Making Breastfeeding Easier
Ensure that your baby has latched on to the areola (the pink or brown
part around the nipple base) and not the tip of the nipple, which is the
main reason for cracked and sore nipples. If it continues to hurt, de-latch
and get the baby to latch back on again.

or brown
part around the nipple base) and not the tip of the nipple, which is the
main reason for cracked and sore nipples. If it continues to hurt, de-latch
and get the baby to latch back on again.
If the position you are using is not working, try another one. There are
different ways of breastfeeding, such as the cradle hold, the football
hold or the cross-cradle hold, lying on your side or laid-back
breastfeeding after a C-section (see p. 82–83).
If there is difficulty latching on, get your partner or your mother to gently
hold open the baby’s mouth and manoeuvre the breast into it.
If your breasts are sore, you can ask for a nipple shield as a temporary
measure. You can use ointments containing lanolin to soothe the breast.

Best Feeding Positions
The best position is the one in which both the baby and the mother are
comfortable. Here are a few of the common ones:
1. 
Cradle position (most commonly used position).
2. 
Cross-cradle position (can be used for smaller babies, where you need to
support the head better; some mothers prefer to use this for babies who
struggle to latch).
3. 
Football position or clutch (I have often found that when babies have
difficulty in latching on to a particular breast, they finally take to it with
the football hold. Once he is a little older, you can move him on to the
cradle position).
4. 
Reclining/side-lying down position (it is a convenient way to feed in the
night; importantly, ensure you don’t fall asleep, else there is a chance of
rolling over the baby).

Latching
1. Prior to feeding, make sure your hands are clean.
2. Hold your breast with the thumb above and your fingers below the breast
and touch the nipple to the baby’s lips or cheek.

3. The baby will usually turn to grab hold of the breast – this is called the
rooting reflex.
4. When the baby opens the mouth wide, put the nipple deep into her
mouth, so at least an inch of the breast – the whole nipple and as much of
the areola – is inside. If the latch is correct, breastfeeding should not be
painful.
5. You should be able to hear the baby gulp and swallow, and your breast
should feel empty at the end of the feed. You should not be left with sore
or cracked nipples.
6. If you have an incorrect latch, you will not hear the normal gulping
sounds and the breast will pain. In such a case, de-latch and try all over
again. For a breastfeeding video tutorial, the Stanford Children’s Hospital
breastfeeding education site is extremely helpful

ounds and the breast will pain. In such a case, de-latch and try all over
again. For a breastfeeding video tutorial, the Stanford Children’s Hospital
breastfeeding education site is extremely helpful
(www.firstdroplets.com). It is important to get the correct latch because
if the baby doesn’t empty the breast properly, it can lead to engorgement
and sometimes an infection called mastitis. Incomplete emptying of the
breast means the baby gets less milk and she is constantly feeding.

LATCHING
24–36 hours
Over the next 24–36 hours, babies tend to sleep a lot. This is because labour and
delivery are tiring for the baby. The maximum that one can allow the baby to
sleep is 4 hours at a stretch. During this time, as the baby is sleepy, one may
find it difficult to figure out a pattern for feedings, so it may be short and
disorganised. Try and feed the baby every 2–3 hours.
24–36 hours till 3 days
The baby will suddenly be more awake now and feed a lot. She may feed 8–12
times in a day. Sometimes, babies feed frequently – every 1 or 2 hours – and
sleep for 3–4 hours at a stretch. Each feed may last 30–45 minutes. At this time,
it is normal for babies to demand feeds more often, and this is what helps in the
production of milk, leading to the let-down reflex (see p. 87).

etch. Each feed may last 30–45 minutes. At this time,
it is normal for babies to demand feeds more often, and this is what helps in the
production of milk, leading to the let-down reflex (see p. 87).
Between 3 and 7 days
Mothers will feel like their breasts are heavier before the feed and empty after
the feed. When the baby has a good feed, you can hear at least 6–8 gulps when
she feeds. Babies are more settled during this phase. They could have 8–12

feeds in 24 hours and the gaps between feeds could be anything between 1 hour
and 3 hours. Sometimes, babies tend to feed several times in a few hours – this is
called cluster feeding. After this, they can sleep for 4–5 hours – this is also
normal. By the end of the week, you should be able to get into a schedule of
feeds every 2.5 to 3 hours.
Changes in the Breasts
The breast can go through numerous changes during preganancy and while
breastfeeding. So let’s put it all down in order.
Pregnancy
Towards the end of your pregnancy, there may be some secretion called
colostrum. This does not happen to every pregnant woman but if it does happen,
it’s nothing to feel alarmed about. If you are feeling uncomfortable, you can
express and remove some of it.
Postpartum (Post-delivery)

does not happen to every pregnant woman but if it does happen,
it’s nothing to feel alarmed about. If you are feeling uncomfortable, you can
express and remove some of it.
Postpartum (Post-delivery)
Milk doesn’t set in till the first 2–5 days. Most mothers will experience
a feeling of heaviness, called the let-down reflex. The heaviness is
there before feeding, and once the baby has had a good feed, the breast
will feel empty. Sometimes, when the mother is feeding on one side,
there may be milk leaking out or dripping from the other breast. These
changes usually then settle down over the next few weeks: the breast
may not feel as full and one may not have any leaking, but that doesn’t
mean there is less milk production.

changes usually then settle down over the next few weeks: the breast
may not feel as full and one may not have any leaking, but that doesn’t

Some mothers usually feel sore, or a pulling sensation, when the baby
starts to feed. However, this should settle in a few days. If the nipples
have a burning sensation every time you feed, it may be because of a
yeast infection. Yeast is a fungal infection that usually grows in dark,
warm, moist places. It looks like a curdy deposit on the baby’s mouth

or tongue. You may feel burning and itching on your nipple and pain
when you’re feeding. Get the baby checked by a paediatrician, who
may prescribe an anti-fungal lotion to be applied on the baby’s mouth
and a cream for your breast. Remember to wipe off the cream from the
breast before feeding.
Tips: Beating Yeast Infection
Wash hands thoroughly with hot, soapy water and dry them properly
before feeding the baby and changing his diaper.
Wash yours and your baby’s clothes in the hottest water possible. Iron
your baby’s clothes before use.
If using a pacifier or a nipple shield, sterilise it properly before each use.
While being treated for the infection, don’t freeze and store your breast
milk.
Decrease consumption of sugar and increase intake of vegetables and

pple shield, sterilise it properly before each use.
While being treated for the infection, don’t freeze and store your breast
milk.
Decrease consumption of sugar and increase intake of vegetables and
garlic. Garlic is known to help fight these infections.
Engorgement
Most mothers will feel some mild engorgement between the third and the fifth
day. This soon settles when milk comes in and the let-down reflex becomes
active. Once the baby starts feeding better and more frequently, the feeling of
engorgement usually settles.
Abnormal engorgement is when it remains severely painful and doesn’t
settle even after feeding the baby. It can be associated with fever and redness on
the overlying skin.
REASONS
Incorrect latch: Due to improper latching, the breasts are not emptied

with each feed; hence, milk accumulates.
Excess milk production: Some women just produce more milk than
the baby can handle. With proper positioning, and as the baby’s appetite
increases, this settles down.
Blocked duct: In this case, the engorgement is more localised, and one
area of the breast is hard and painful.
Too many top-ups: Offering the baby more top-up feeds and not
allowing the baby to breastfeed completely also results in engorgement.
TREATMENT
It is better to tackle this early in the following ways:
Making sure the latch is proper because if the breast is not emptied it
will just compound the problem. Take help from the nursing staff or a
lactation consultant before leaving the hospital. Your baby’s
paediatrician is not going to be able to help you with this on the phone.

lactation consultant before leaving the hospital. Your baby’s
paediatrician is not going to be able to help you with this on the phone.
The mother can always express, whether manually or with a pump, to
soften the breast so that the baby is able to latch more comfortably. If
she feels that the breast is engorged even after feeding, she can express
it then.
Take a hot shower or apply a hot towel to improve the flow of milk.
Cold compress or an ice pack may be used to reduce swelling.
Analgesics like paracetamol or brufen can be taken to deal with the
pain, after consultation with your doctor.
It is important to continuously feed and express to treat engorgement

Engorgement can also happen when the baby is weaned and each breastfeed
is replaced with solid food. However, as it happens gradually, most mothers do
not experience uncomfortable engorgement. Occasionally, though, it can be

severe and painful. You can take analgesics and may need to express small
amounts in order to relieve the pain, but don’t empty the breast as it will fill up
again.
Mastitis (Breast infection)
Although it is quite rare, mastitis is an extremely distressing and painful
condition.
Sore nipples and breast engorgement are precursors to mastitis. When
nipples are sore, bacteria can enter the breast and stagnant milk is an
ideal medium for bacteria to multiply.
It can be a localised infection associated with a blocked duct, where
there will be tenderness in one area of the breast and redness of the
overlying skin. The mother may also have flu-like symptoms.
When it is associated with engorgement, the whole breast may be
involved and as a result become hard and painful. The whole skin over

f the
overlying skin. The mother may also have flu-like symptoms.
When it is associated with engorgement, the whole breast may be
involved and as a result become hard and painful. The whole skin over
the breast may be red and the mother may be systemically unwell with
fever.
Mild infections are treated with antibiotics and analgesics and you can
continue to breastfeed, as keeping the milk flowing will prevent the
spread of infection.
In severe cases, if there is formation of an abscess, it will need to be
drained (the mother may be asked to do an ultrasound for the diagnosis
of an abscess). She may be asked to temporarily stop breastfeeding and
pump to remove the milk. Once the abscess has healed, breastfeeding
may be resumed.

Frequently Asked Questions
QHow long should each breastfeeding session last?

This can vary a lot and is different for each mother and baby. Some babies
feed for nearly half an hour on each side while others are satisfied with just
10 minutes on each side.
Usually, by the end of the first week, babies will be feeding 15–20 minutes
and in the initial phase, both breasts should be offered. This encourages
maximum production of milk. Once lactation has established, you can allow
the baby to feed for 20–25 minutes on one breast. This allows the baby to
get more of the hindmilk which is rich in healthy fats and keeps the baby
more satisfied. At the end of the feed, if the baby still looks hungry, you can
offer the second breast. Remember to start with the opposite breast for the
next feed. Some mothers have told me they use methods like putting a rubber

e feed, if the baby still looks hungry, you can
offer the second breast. Remember to start with the opposite breast for the
next feed. Some mothers have told me they use methods like putting a rubber
band on the hand to remind them which breast they fed from last – this really
helps when you are sleep-deprived.
QFor how long should I feed my baby at night?
For the first 6–8 weeks, you will be feeding your baby 3 hourly at night
(once every 3 hours). After this, you can slowly discourage the baby from
taking the middle-of-the-night feed around 2 a.m. to 3 a.m. If you are lucky,
you may find that by the time your baby is 3 months of age, he may go 6
hours without a feed and allow you to sleep for 6 hours.
QHow do I know that my baby is getting enough milk?

If feeding is inadequate, the stools remain green even at the end of the week.
The baby may only pass 1 or 2 stools.
By the end of the week, your baby will be passing urine at least 8–10 times
in a day. If a baby isn’t feeding well, then the urine frequency is less, and it is
more yellow in colour.
Most babies lose about 5%–7% of their body weight in the first week. Any
weight loss more than 10% is indicative of inadequate feed. Most babies
should recover the weight loss and regain their birth weight by 10 days of
life.
QWill using a breast pump help me with breastfeeding?
Your baby is the best breast pump. So, you really don’t need any other breast
pumps. Occasionally, when you have engorgement, then you can use the

Will using a breast pump help me with breastfeeding?
Your baby is the best breast pump. So, you really don’t need any other breast
pumps. Occasionally, when you have engorgement, then you can use the
pump to relieve the engorgement. When you have started work, then you can
use the pump to express and store the milk.
QHow do I store the milk?
Wash your hands with soap and water before expressing milk.
Make sure you sterilize your breast pump. Use cooler bags or sterile
containers to store the milk. These containers should have the date and time
of collection so that you know how soon you can use it.
The containers should be either glass or polypropylene/polyethylene bags.
The containers should be washed in hot, soapy water and rinsed and finally

llection so that you know how soon you can use it.
The containers should be either glass or polypropylene/polyethylene bags.
The containers should be washed in hot, soapy water and rinsed and finally
air dried. When using glass containers, make sure they are freezer-safe ones.
Human milk should not be stored in hospital plastic specimen containers,
such as those used for urine tests and other body fluids.

Freshly expressed breast milk can be stored safely at room temperature,
which is between 10°C and 29°C, for up to 6 hours. If at 27°C–32°C, then
up to 4 hours is safe, but it is best to chill or refrigerate as soon as possible
if you’re not sure whether the milk will be used within 4 hours. In India,
especially in the summer months, it is better to refrigerate milk as soon as it
is pumped.
If breast milk has less bacteria, then even up to 72 hours in the refrigerator is
safe. However, since it is impossible to check whether there is bacteria in the
breast milk or not, it is best to use it within 24–48 hours. It should be stored
at the back of the fridge where the temperature is more stable and not
affected so much by the opening and closing of the fridge door. I normally

est to use it within 24–48 hours. It should be stored
at the back of the fridge where the temperature is more stable and not
affected so much by the opening and closing of the fridge door. I normally
recommend only 24 hours in the refrigerator.

within 3 months. At âˆ’18°C, the milk is safe indefinitely from bacterial
contamination but there could be changes in the quality of milk. For example,
fat, protein and calories reduce after 90 days.
Refrigerated and frozen breast milk may have a different odour than fresh
milk because of the breakdown of triglycerides into fatty acids. It tends to
separate as well but all you need to do is shake it gently to mix it up again.

Never fill the container to the brim since the milk will expand when frozen.
It’s recommended you fill a container to about â…” of its capacity.
Don’t mix the freshly expressed warm milk with the already cool or frozen
milk.
To thaw frozen milk, first place the container in the refrigerator overnight,
then warm it by sitting it in a bowl of warm water, or you can use a waterless
milk warmer.
Microwaving can cause the milk to heat unevenly. It also decreases the
activity of immunological factors in milk and therefore reduces the healthy
properties of milk.

Once frozen milk is thawed and brought to room temperature, it should be
used within 2 hours. Currently, there is no recommendation regarding the
refreezing of thawed breast milk.

my diet can make my baby fussy. Is this

Yes, that’s true. But there is no reason to restrict your diet unless there is a

fussier. Since it takes anywhere from 6 to 24 hours to notice that your baby is
cranky, you need to think about what you ate the past day. Certain foods that
may pose a problem to the baby are cabbage, broccoli, apricots, prunes and
peaches. Some babies may also react to kidney beans and chickpeas.
Especially cruciferous vegetables: if they produce gas in you, then they will
produce gas in the baby too.

It is not about eating sushi, rather it is the raw fish in it that creates a
problem. Anything raw carries an increased risk of food-borne infection

QCan I eat sushi when I’m breastfeeding?
It is not about eating sushi, rather it is the raw fish in it that creates a
problem. Anything raw carries an increased risk of food-borne infection
and, so, it is prudent to try and limit it. The United States Food and Drug
Administration (FDA) also recommends that you eat less than 6 oz (170 gm)
of tuna a week. Tuna contains mercury over the acceptable level; hence, it is
prudent that we eat less of it.

QCan I have a drink while I’m nursing?
Yes, you can have alchohol in limited amount. I suggest you limit your
intake to 1 alcoholic beverage sporadically. Alcohol enters the bloodstream
and goes from there to the breast milk anywhere between 30–90 minutes
after drinking. So, a good rule of thumb is not to feed the baby for at least 2
hours after drinking.
QWhat medications should I avoid while I’m breastfeeding?
There are many medicines that you cannot have while you are breastfeeding.
Also, not all herbs and natural remedies or alternative medicine is safe for
breastfed babies. It is best to check with your doctor.

Surely. While fighting infection, your body is making antibodies which

QI have fever. Can I continue breastfeeding?
Surely. While fighting infection, your body is making antibodies which
then go to your baby and helps her fight infection. If you have a cold, you
can wear a mask and feed the baby. If you have fever immediately after
delivery, please speak to your obstetrician–gynaecologist (OB/GYN) as it
may be indicative of a complication of your delivery.
QAre there any foods and drinks that can increase lactation?
Eating special foods, like specially made laddoos and panjiri and having lots
of ghee, will not increase breast milk output but will definitely make the
mother put on more weight.

QWhat should I do if there is excess milk flow?
If you are feeding when there is excessive let-down, your baby will start
choking. This is because the flow is faster than what the baby can manage to

swallow. You can express some breast milk to reduce the flow, and then put
the baby on the breast. Usually, this settles down on its own and becomes
more manageable for the baby.
QMy baby has GER. What should I do?
Babies with GER need to be fed in a more upright position, but that is
physically impossible when you’re breastfeeding. However, the baby can be
fed at a 45° angle on your lap. Try not to feed her in a lying down position. If
your baby spits up a lot of milk immediately after feeding, you can try
feeding her again. Breast milk gets made as the baby sucks, so you don’t
need to worry about your child going hungry. Yet, if the baby is still hungry,
you may have to give some expressed breast milk or top up with formula

Breastfeeding: The First Year
Birth to 2 weeks
Your baby may appear to feed every 1–2 hours in the first 2 weeks, and may add
up to 10–12 feeds per day. She may also do cluster feeding where she feeds
every half an hour for 2–3 hours and then go off to sleep for another 2–3 hours.
If your baby is feeding more frequently than every 90 minutes, it may be
because she is nursing for comfort. Sucking is extremely soothing for babies,
but it is important to weigh them to be sure they are getting enough milk. This is
because if your baby is not gaining weight adequately, your doctor may decide
to give you some medication or tips to increase your lactation. Breast milk
operates in accordance with the supply and demand phenomenon. So the more
often you feed the baby, the more milk you will make.

ive you some medication or tips to increase your lactation. Breast milk
operates in accordance with the supply and demand phenomenon. So the more
often you feed the baby, the more milk you will make.
2 weeks to 2 months
By now your milk supply is well established and your baby will probably be
feeding 2–3 hourly. Your baby would be gaining weight adequately and
occasionally, sleeping through the night. Please don’t wake him up and don’t
jinx it by telling your friends! If you feel full and uncomfortable, don’t wake up
the sleeping baby – just pump out the milk and store it.
2–4 months
A more regular feeding and sleeping pattern has been established. Your baby
may only wake up once or twice for a feed.
4–6 months
Most babies have well-established feeding patterns by now and may only take 6

egular feeding and sleeping pattern has been established. Your baby
may only wake up once or twice for a feed.
4–6 months
Most babies have well-established feeding patterns by now and may only take 6
feeds in a day. You may even have started complementary solid foods by now
(see p. 106).

6–9 months
The number of breastfeeds will be steadily reducing as the baby gets bigger and
due to the complementary feeds being introduced. By 9 months, most babies
should be sleeping through the night and not waking up for a night feed.
9–12 months
By now, the baby should be eating more solids and drinking less. Your baby
may have decided to totally wean off breastfeeding. But if you’re continuing to
breastfeed, ideally, it should only be 2 or 3 feeds a day. You should be switching
to full-fat regular milk for the baby which you will serve in a cup, not a bottle.
The baby needs the equivalent of 2 cups of milk in 24 hours.

Feeding Premature Babies
If babies are born extremely preterm, they’re likely to be in the NICU. It is
important that these babies get breast milk as soon as possible. As soon as the
mother recovers from the delivery, she will be asked to express milk using a
pump every 3 hours, like she’s feeding a baby. In the initial few days, it may be
very small amounts, but even that will be enough for preterm babies. A
newborn’s tummy at birth is only about the size of a small almond. This is a
very stressful period for the mother and she will need a lot of support.
Going to the NICU and holding the baby will go a long way in producing
adequate milk. The milk is then usually fed to the baby by a tube.

ful period for the mother and she will need a lot of support.
Going to the NICU and holding the baby will go a long way in producing
adequate milk. The milk is then usually fed to the baby by a tube.
Once the baby is 32 weeks or older, the sucking reflex develops. Most
NICUs will try some direct breastfeeding which is called non-nutritive sucking.
This helps increase mother’s breast milk, but the baby is not going to be able to
get too much milk from this sucking. Expressed breast milk is then usually
offered using a traditional cup (called ‘paladai’ in Tamil or ‘jhinuk’ in Bengali).
As the mother feels the sucking getting stronger, more direct breastfeeds are
attempted.

Pumping while away from your baby can be challenging. You could try

This can help, even though you are apart.
As your baby matures, you will be asked to put her to the breast for short

have better control over latching and positioning.
As the baby grows stronger with each passing day, you yourself will feel the

baby to suck for longer periods. Initially, you will be asked to supplement the

Feeding Twins
Usually, most mothers who’ve had twins can produce enough milk for the twin
babies for the first 3–4 months. They find it more difficult to manage to feed the
babies than to produce breast milk. Here are different ways to feed twins.
In sequence
One feeds from one breast, empties the breast and if still hungry is given top-up
formula. The second twin is given the second breast.
Alternate breastfeed
One baby is allowed to feed from both breasts in a feed and no top-up is given.
The second one is given formula. For the next feed, the one who got formula

ven the second breast.
Alternate breastfeed
One baby is allowed to feed from both breasts in a feed and no top-up is given.
The second one is given formula. For the next feed, the one who got formula
will feed from both breasts and the one who was breastfed will get top-up. This
type of feeding needs a bit of monitoring, and you will need to keep a track of
which baby got formula and which one a breastfeed.
Simultaneous feed
Using the football hold, both babies are fed simultaneously from each breast.

Soya-based formulas are used for children with cow’s milk protein allergy.
Here, the soya protein replaces the cow’s milk protein that is present in standard
formulas. Lactose, which is the milk sugar, is replaced in these formulas with
either sucrose or maltodextrins. Hence, it can be used by children who develop
lactose intolerance, especially after a gastrointestinal illness.
Specialised formulas
Hypoallergenic formulas: These are made from extensively
hydrolysed cow’s milk protein and are useful for children with cow’s
milk protein intolerance. These are not so freely available in India.

Formula Instead of Breast Milk
Generally, owing to the risk of infection and the high cost, formula feeding is
discouraged. There will be situations when the mother is unable to exclusively
breastfeed the child and has no other option.
The advantage of formula over fresh milk is that it is specially prepared to be
as close to human milk in terms of fats, carbohydrates and nutrient content. The
AAP recommends that formula feeds – and not cow’s milk – should be given to
children less than 1 year of age because of iron deficiency, anaemia, and
cow’s milk protein allergy. The WHO supports the use of fresh milk in the
developing world in children over 6 months.

Since the risk of gastrointestinal infections is quite high with bottle
feeding, cup and spoon feeds are encouraged. The baby should move to cup
feeding as soon as possible. This should be ideally attempted at 6 months and
the baby should have transitioned to cup feeding completely by 9 months.
Sterilisation
Bottle feeds are easy and convenient for mothers, but
sterilisation is very important. You need at least 4–6 bottles.
They need to be cleaned with soap and sterilised either by
boiling or steam sterilisation or using sterilising liquids or
tablets.

Boiling is one of the best methods of sterilisations and the
bottles, as well as the bottle-nipple, should be boiled for at
least 10 minutes before every feed. If you find this difficult to
do, then it is better to use a cup and a spoon.

Preparation of formula
Wash hands thoroughly. The water that is used to make the formula can be either
tap water which is boiled or filtered water which is boiled. The water should
be stored in a sterile vessel. The required amount, i.e., 30 ml of water, is put into
the bottle and one scoop of formula is added. This is the standard dilution but
different formulas may have different dilution, so it always better to read the
instructions. The temperature can be checked by putting a few drops of milk
on the back of the hand or the inside of your forearm to check that it is not too
hot. Once you prepare the formula at room temperature, it should be consumed
within 1 hour. If it is kept in the refrigerator, you can use it up to 6 hours.

Never microwave the milk as it heats up unevenly, creating hotspots. You
can always warm up refrigerated milk by keeping the bottle standing in hot

When travelling, it is better to carry the water separately and the powder

QHow much milk should I make if my baby is bottle-feeding?
Unlike breastfed babies who feed until they are done, with bottle feeding, the
general rule of thumb is that there should be a little bit of milk remaining in
the bottle at the end of the feed. This way you know that your baby is full.
So, if your baby takes 60 ml at a time, then you make 90 ml.

When you are feeding with a cup and spoon, you will need to give the milk

Weaning: Starting Solids
Normally, we start weaning (introduce solid foods) around 6 months of age,
which means your baby will be taking small amounts of solid food.
I would advise parents to prepare food at home, rather than give
commercial baby food. Sure, off-the-shelf food is convenient and can be used
when travelling, or when there is no time to prepare food, but try to make
commercial packaged foods exceptions, not the norm.
SOME SIMPLE GUIDELINES:
You can try any food, except raw food or honey.
You can start any time after the baby completes 5 months: once your
baby starts showing interest in the food that you are eating, when the
baby is able to sit supported and when the baby’s head is fairly steady.
Offer 1 ingredient at a time. For example: 1 fruit for a few days in order

interest in the food that you are eating, when the
baby is able to sit supported and when the baby’s head is fairly steady.
Offer 1 ingredient at a time. For example: 1 fruit for a few days in order
to identify any food intolerance.
You can use seasoning or spice, but ideally, no salt till 1 year of age.
Eating is a developmental milestone, and the child will start eating small
amounts initially till she learns to chew, move the food around in her
mouth and finally swallow. This is usually figured out by 9 months of
age. By 9 months, babies should be eating 3 solid meals a day and the
liquid/milk intake would be far less now.
We need to encourage babies to eat more iron-rich foods as breast milk
is a relatively poor source of iron.

Frequently Asked Questions
QWhen is my baby ready for solids?
Babies are ready to eat when ‘they know what to do with their food’. Make
your baby sit at the dining table with you. If your child is watching what
you eat, offer him a small bite. You can literally offer your baby anything
other than honey. If your baby swallows the food, he is ready for weaning.
There is a great deal of variability amongst babies when it comes to weaning.
The bottom line is, don’t rush. As of now, milk is still the priority.
The advice on this topic is inconsistent. The breastfeeding group will say
‘wait till 6 months before solids’, while some paediatricians – including the
AAP – recommend starting solids between 4 and 6 months.
Starting solids will not make your baby sleep through the night. Some say

ll 6 months before solids’, while some paediatricians – including the
AAP – recommend starting solids between 4 and 6 months.
Starting solids will not make your baby sleep through the night. Some say
that having a full tummy will help the baby sleep well. But sleeping through
the night has more to do with your baby’s developmental stage and her being
ready for it, rather than a full tummy. Babies weaned before 4 months may
develop obesity later in life.
QWhat is the first solid food I should offer?
This, again, is a controversial topic. Some paediatricians will recommend
mashed vegetables for the first few weeks and then adding dal (lentils) and
rice to the mixture.
Many paediatricians in the Western world, where meat and chicken

constitute a significant part of the typical diet, would recommend including

If there is no family history of severe eczema or any kind of food allergy in

the family, babies can be given whatever they want, whenever they want.
Of course, you shouldn’t give any fatty, fried, salty or sugary foods to your
baby. Keep junk food away from him.
QHow and when should I give my baby her first solid meal?
Chances are that if you try the first solid meal when your baby is hungry, she
may get frustrated and not eat at all. It is advisable to give her regular milk,
and then offer the food as a snack between 2 milk feeds. Once your baby is
happy taking small amounts, you can gradually increase it, and the solid food
will replace the milk feed.
Ideally, while being fed, your baby should be seated on a high chair. But if
the baby cannot sit upright, or hold the head steady, then it’s a sign she’s not
yet ready for solid food intake.

QWhat is baby-led weaning?
Advocates of this method of weaning feel that babies over 6 months do not
need to be spoon-fed or eat puréed food. These babies are given bite-sized
pieces of solid food such as cucumber sticks, pieces of banana, soft-cooked
chicken and boiled eggs. They may not eat a lot and may gag initially, but
they usually do not choke. As babies are encouraged to select and eat on
their own, they end up trying a wide variety of foods. The proponents of
baby-led weaning believe that with this method, fussy eating and food battles

e. As babies are encouraged to select and eat on
their own, they end up trying a wide variety of foods. The proponents of
baby-led weaning believe that with this method, fussy eating and food battles
are less likely to happen later on as the baby decides what and how much to
eat.
Most paediatricians are concerned that with this method the baby may not
consume adequate iron-rich nutrition and may become anaemic by the age of
9 months.
QHow often do I feed my baby solid food?

We start with 1 solid meal at around 5–6 months of age and increase it to 2
meals by 7–8 months. By 9 months of age, babies should be having 3 solid
meals a day.
Until 9 months, milk should form a major part of your baby’s diet. After 9
months, the proportion of solid food should increase, and the quantity of milk
should decrease.
QCan I give my child fruit juice?
It’s always better to give fresh fruit rather than juice, because the fibre in the
fruit is good for the baby. After 9 months, small amounts of fruit juice, such
as sweet lime, orange, pomegranate and apple, may be offered between
meals, once in a while.
Don’t give your baby any packaged fruit juice. They may contain
preservatives, additives, and some can be very sugary. If you want to give

Don’t give your baby any packaged fruit juice. They may contain

QHow much water should I give my baby?
Water is essential once you start solid food intake. Many babies do not take
to water, but to begin with you can offer a few sips 2–3 times a day.
Exclusively breastfed babies get most of their water requirement through
milk and do not require much external water intake – even in hot weather. To
quench their thirst, they tend to feed more often.
If your baby is passing pale urine, then your baby is getting adequate water.

If your baby is passing pale urine, then your baby is getting adequate water.
Always offer boiled and cooled, or filtered water to your baby. Don’t add

Q My baby has started eating vegetables and now he is looking
yellow. What is the reason?

Sometimes, if you give your baby too many vegetables containing vitamin A
(such as carrots, spinach and sweet potato), the skin may turn yellow. This
is termed carotinemia. This is not alarming and will settle down the minute
you reduce the intake of these vegetables. In the meantime, include other
seasonal vegetables in the diet. This also brings variety and encourages
healthy eating.
QCan I feed my baby organic baby food?
It’s up to you, as this is a personal, lifestyle choice. However, if you want to
feed your baby organic food, it’s better to buy raw organic ingredients and
prepare the food yourself, instead of buying commercially prepared organic
food.

QHow will I know if my baby has a food allergy?
Most severe food allergies show up between a few minutes to a few hours
after the meal. It can range from scary rashes all over the body to profuse
vomiting and bloody diarrhoea.
Allergic reactions are rarely subtle and are usually caused by a chemical
released in the body called histamine. The baby may also develop urticaria
or hives, which look like large mosquito bites.
In extreme cases, a baby can get an anaphylactic reaction. There may be
swelling in the airway and loss of consciousness. This is life-threatening,
and you may need to take the baby to the hospital. However, this is rare in

to, he can have cramps in his stomach and bloating. This usually happens
every time he eats a particular food or food group.
You will probably not give this particular food to your baby again, but your
baby is unlikely to develop a life-threatening or anaphylactic reaction if he’s
exposed to this food.
For example, an adult with lactose intolerance will not be able to tolerate
milk in his coffee, and will probably develop a side-effect such as bloating,
but not an allergic reaction such as urticaria.

In eczema, there is a broken skin barrier where children are exposed to food
proteins such as peanuts. Here, the immune system is not so welcoming and,
in fact, sensitises the body and leads to a food allergy. On removing the
offending or concerned food from the diet, the eczema is likely to improve.
Thus, if the eczema is controlled by using good emollients, thereby reducing
the itching, the food allergy actually gets better.

allergens through the skin can lead to an allergy, consuming this as food at
an early age may result in tolerance because the gut immune system

QIs Coeliac disease a type of allergy?
Coeliac disease is an abnormal immune response to the wheat protein
gluten. It is quite common in the Western population; however, the incidence
of this disease in the Indian populace has been steadily increasing over the
years. It is a combination of genetics, where it is passed down the family,
and a sensitivity to gluten.
This leads to injury and ulceration of the intestinal lining, leading to
difficulty in digestion of food containing gluten. Most gluten-sensitive
children present to their paediatrician with symptoms of chronic diarrhoea,
failure to thrive, vomiting, bloating and anaemia. In some rare cases, there

food containing gluten. Most gluten-sensitive
children present to their paediatrician with symptoms of chronic diarrhoea,
failure to thrive, vomiting, bloating and anaemia. In some rare cases, there
are no symptoms. These symptoms show once you start solid foods
containing gluten, such as wheat, barley and rye.
These children will undergo a screening blood test and, if it is positive, they
will need an endoscopy and biopsy. Then, they will be sent to a dietician
who will aid the family in understanding which food groups need to be
avoided. The dietary restrictions need to be strictly followed, otherwise the
baby will continue to be symptomatic and this can lead to faltering of growth.
QWhat tests will the baby need to identify a food allergy?

Elimination: In cases like lactose intolerance, you can eliminate the
food from the diet and look for improvement in the next 4–6 weeks.
Skin testing (skin prick tests): They detect a true allergic response to
different antigens.
Rast test: This is blood test that detects a body’s immunoglobulin E
(IGE) levels. These are usually raised but, unfortunately, not all food
allergens will give a raised IGE response. These tests are quite useful in
predicting allergies to milk, eggs, peanuts and fish.
QAre there any foods that my baby shouldn’t eat?
Honey shouldn’t be given to babies under a year old because it may
contain spores of clostridium botulinum. This can cause a fatal
disease called botulism.
Foods that could be choking hazards including small pieces of raw

en to babies under a year old because it may
contain spores of clostridium botulinum. This can cause a fatal
disease called botulism.
Foods that could be choking hazards including small pieces of raw
carrot, popcorn, potato chips, and nuts, fruits with seeds, whole
raisins and whole grapes. These must be started only when the baby is
2 or 3 years old, i.e., when she can chew well.
Artificial sweeteners
Junk food
QMy 1-year-old only eats puréed food. Should I be worried?
This is called texture aversion. As long as your baby’s development is
normal, it’s not a big worry. Things will improve with time – just be patient
and keep offering foods with a variety of textures to encourage healthy
eating.
QCan I feed my baby fish?

Certain fish like shark and king mackerel may contain high levels of
mercury. Notwithstanding, it is difficult to recommend a fish that contains
relatively safe levels of mercury. Levels of mercury in fish vary from region
to region. You must check this first-hand in your region.
REMEMBER: Breastmilk and formula have 20 calories per 30 ml of feed.
Hence, when your baby drinks 240 ml of milk, he gets 160 calories. If he eats

the liquid nutrition until your baby is eating sufficient solid food (at least 3–4

Potty, Spit-Up, GERD and Other Problems
As paediatricians, we don’t need to look at the stool to make a diagnosis of
gastroenteritis. Yes, of course, it’s useful to know if there is any blood in the
potty, but we can always get a stool test done in the lab. So please don’t send
your paediatrician close-up photos of your baby’s potty – unless you’re asked for
it. Your paediatrician won’t appreciate looking at potty pictures during her lunch
break when she decides to answer her messages.
Let’s look at what constitutes spit-up and the more serious gastro-
oesophageal reflux disease, or GERD, by answering some of your frequently
asked questions. Acid reflux happens when the contents of your stomach come
back up into your oesophagus, which is a tube that carries food from the throat to

RD, by answering some of your frequently
asked questions. Acid reflux happens when the contents of your stomach come
back up into your oesophagus, which is a tube that carries food from the throat to
the stomach. At the junction where the oesophagus meets the stomach lies a ring
of muscle that normally opens when you swallow. This muscle is known as the
lower oesophageal sphincter (LES). After eating or drinking, if this sphincter
does not close completely, stomach contents along with the gastric juices can
come up into the oesophagus.
In babies, the LES may be weak or underdeveloped. In fact, more than half
of all infants experience acid reflux to some degree – it usually peaks at around 4
months and fades away around 12–14 months. GERD is a much more severe
form of this.

Frequently Asked Questions

QWhat does a breastfed baby’s potty look like?
On the first–second day, your baby’s potty is called meconium. It is usually
black or dark green in colour and very tarry in texture. As the baby starts to
feed, the colour and texture changes.
Once the baby has cleared his meconium, usually by the third or fourth day,
the potty starts to get more liquid and yellower in colour. Babies will pass a
stool after every feed or between feeds. They could do 8 to 12 potties in a
day, the colour varying from light green to mustard yellow to brown. After 4
to 6 weeks, your baby’s potty frequency will drastically decrease, and he may
do potty only once in 3 or 4 days. This is also normal.
QWhat does a formula-fed baby’s potty look like?

o brown. After 4
to 6 weeks, your baby’s potty frequency will drastically decrease, and he may
do potty only once in 3 or 4 days. This is also normal.
QWhat does a formula-fed baby’s potty look like?
‘Formula potty’ tends to be greener, thicker and pastier than breastmilk
potty. It’s also smellier.
Formula-fed babies do potty less frequently than breastfed babies. The potty
is initially only 3 to 4 times a day. But by 4 to 6 weeks, this interval may
reduce to once in 2 days.

no blood mixed with the stool. The presence of blood should always be

not sterilising the bottles well if you are using a bottle. So any time you
notice blood in the baby’s stool, please speak to your doctor as it will
need investigation and treatment.
Your baby may appear to have a tummy ache followed by a passage of
red-currant, jelly-like stool. This condition is called intussusception, in
which one part of the intestine telescopes (folds and collapses) into the

Q I have started solids for my baby and now she is constipated. What
should I do?
Some fruits like banana and rice cereal can make the potty very hard. To
counter this, you can add mashed prunes, dates, oatmeal and porridge
(dalia) to the baby’s diet. Also, give your baby regular sips of water through
the day.

QWhen do I start potty training?
In India, grandparents typically ask you to start potty training the child when
she is 3 months of age. Realistically speaking, this is a developmental
milestone which children do not reach until they are 2 years old.

Other opinions state that babies as young as 6 months do provide subtle clues
when they need to poop, and parents should respond to these cues by making
the baby sit on the pot every time they feel that the baby has the urge to pee
or do potty. This is more conditioning than actual potty training. If you are
overzealous at this stage, it may all backfire and your baby may start to hold
both urine and stool. Then retraining them will be an uphill task. So, go with
the flow. If your baby appears to be ready, then by all means put him on the
toilet seat after meals or when he shows signs, but at no point should you
force the baby to sit on the seat. There are some indicators that your baby is
ready to be trained:
She seems interested in the toilet, potty etc.
She may want to watch you go.

Posseting, Regurgitations (GER)
Most babies tend to posset (spit-up) small amounts of milk after feeding. This is
because the muscle at the lower end of the oesophagus (food canal) and the
stomach is loose.
Milk, being liquid, easily travels from the stomach back into the oesophagus.
This is otherwise called acid reflux, or GER. This is harmless and usually
settles down by 6 months (sometimes 9 months) of age as the sphincter muscle

vels from the stomach back into the oesophagus.
This is otherwise called acid reflux, or GER. This is harmless and usually
settles down by 6 months (sometimes 9 months) of age as the sphincter muscle
tightens. It may appear to you that as the baby gets older, he spits up larger
amounts. Do not worry – this is only because he takes larger amounts of feed as
he gets older.
GERD
This is usually associated with irritation of the oesophagus, making the baby

very irritable and reluctant to feed. The baby becomes a fussy feeder and
occasionally appears to be in pain. The baby may also have respiratory
problems like choking and wheezing. These babies usually don’t gain weight
very well and will need to be on medication to reduce their symptoms.
Symptoms
Your baby will spit up large amounts of milk and, hence, will not gain
weight regularly.
She may be a very unsettled baby and cry most of the time, even if she
doesn’t spit up much. This is because the stomach acid irritates the
lower end of the oesophagus.
Sometimes your baby’s projectile vomit may be large. The baby’s
body may slowly build up this large volume of vomit over the course of
many days. The vomit is usually always milk and is not yellow in

Sometimes your baby’s projectile vomit may be large. The baby’s
body may slowly build up this large volume of vomit over the course of
many days. The vomit is usually always milk and is not yellow in
colour. It may be due to a condition called pyloric stenosis. This
condition occurs due to the narrowing of the junction between the
stomach and small intestine because of pylorus (thickening of the
muscle in that region). The treatment for this condition is surgery. So,
if your baby has projectile vomit after eating food, you must consult
your doctor.
Most parents may have already tried over-the-counter medicines like colic-
aid drops, switched formulas, or taken off milk from the mother’s diet
before consulting a paediatrician. Certain features that most babies with GERD
have:

ried over-the-counter medicines like colic-
aid drops, switched formulas, or taken off milk from the mother’s diet
before consulting a paediatrician. Certain features that most babies with GERD
have:
They are uncomfortable lying down.
They cry during feeds and shortly after feeding.
They may arch their backs.
Very often, they don’t actually spit up much milk as the milk may not
actually rise up to the mouth but remain in the lower end of the

oesophagus.
These babies are uncomfortable and unhappy all day long.
In stark contrast, colicky babies usually have a set time of day when they are
unhappy, say from 9 p.m. to 12 a.m. This usually starts from around 3 weeks of
age to 3 months. Your paediatrician may administer a probiotic to your baby to
relieve these symptoms.
Babies with milk allergies may also be fussy and irritable, but in addition
will have very loose potty which may have mucus and blood in it. These
children may also have eczema.
Colic
The C word is what every new mother dreads. If I were to believe the patients’
mothers who come to see me, there is no baby without colic. Most babies cry for
short periods in the day and that is perfectly normal; this is their only method of

If I were to believe the patients’
mothers who come to see me, there is no baby without colic. Most babies cry for
short periods in the day and that is perfectly normal; this is their only method of
communication. Soon, most mothers will be able to figure out the reason for the
crying – whether it is because of a wet diaper or the baby is hungry or she just
wants a cuddle.
The definition of colic is when a baby who is not sick or hungry cries for
more than 3 hours of the day more than 3 days a week for more than 3 weeks.
This condition is a mystery, but most experts agree on a few things:
Colic is likely to start around 2 weeks of age in a full-term baby and
later in a preterm baby.
It almost always disappears by 3 to 4 months of age.

a mystery, but most experts agree on a few things:
Colic is likely to start around 2 weeks of age in a full-term baby and
later in a preterm baby.
It almost always disappears by 3 to 4 months of age.
It can happen equally to either sex and equally to breastfed and bottle-
fed babies.
Kids who have colic grow up no differently from other babies.
CAUSES:

Nobody really knows why this happens. Some experts believe that it is caused
by an immature gastrointestinal tract or digestive system. At birth, the baby
has no microbes in the intestine. These are needed for a healthy digestive
system. Once the healthy bacteria colonize the intestine, the colic improves.
Breastmilk is laden with these healthy bacteria.
Gas is also thought to produce colic. When babies cry, they take in a lot of
air and make themselves more uncomfortable.
Overstimulation and sensitivity to light and noise may be another cause.
Some also say it could be an early form of migraine. Children with migraine
are more likely to have experienced infantile colic compared to children without
migraine.
SYMPTOMS:

may be another cause.
Some also say it could be an early form of migraine. Children with migraine
are more likely to have experienced infantile colic compared to children without
migraine.
SYMPTOMS:
Infants often show signs of colic at the same time every day, usually in the
evening. They cry for no apparent reason. They cry like they are in pain. Fists
are clenched, legs are drawn up, backs arched, and they usually turn very red.
There is no particular test to diagnose colic.
GER can mimic colic. So, if you have an unusually fussy baby, it’s important
to get her checked to rule out GER. These babies are irritable and miserable all
day and night, especially around feeding. They usually outgrow this by 1 year.
Cow’s milk protein allergy may also cause colic.
TREATMENT:

o rule out GER. These babies are irritable and miserable all
day and night, especially around feeding. They usually outgrow this by 1 year.
Cow’s milk protein allergy may also cause colic.
TREATMENT:
If you suspect overstimulation, respond promptly to her crying.
Studies have shown that responding promptly to the baby’s cry will
reduce the crying in the long run.
Eliminate excitement, restrict visitors and limit exposure to
stimulating experiences, especially in the early evening. Try and stay
clear of stimuli that seem to offend your baby. Try to create a calm
atmosphere, speak softly in soothing tones, dim the lights.
If you suspect it’s due to gas, place her on her tummy on your lap or
against your shoulder where the tummy is pressed. Gently rub her back

till she calms down. Try burping your baby. This may relieve the gas.
Try anti-gas medicine. Your doctor may prescribe simethicone drops,
though there is no definitive research to show that it helps with colic.
Probiotics are now recommended for colic. Studies have used bacteria
like L. reuteri or bifidobacter. Between the probiotic group and the
simethicone group, there was a definite reduction of colic in the
probiotic group. I am not saying that it will work all the time, but it’s
definitely worth a try.
Probiotic drops are mixed in expressed breastmilk or formula,
according to the amount recommended by your doctor, and placed on
the tongue.
Since what you eat appears in your breastmilk, if your baby is colicky,
it’s important to watch what you eat. Cruciferous vegetables, such as

unt recommended by your doctor, and placed on
the tongue.
Since what you eat appears in your breastmilk, if your baby is colicky,
it’s important to watch what you eat. Cruciferous vegetables, such as
cauliflower, citrus fruits, milk, peanuts, eggs, soy and fish, can cause
trouble for your baby.
If your child is on formula, then your doctor may prescribe those that
are hypoallergenic.
Acid Reflux
Acid reflux is usually treated with an antacid and an anti-vomiting medicine
initially. Despite that, if your baby continues to be symptomatic, he may be
given the second group of medicines that are used to treat GERD – these are
called Proton Pump Inhibitors (PPIs). They may need to be given this for a
few weeks or months, based on your paediatrician’s medical opinion, or you

medicines that are used to treat GERD – these are
called Proton Pump Inhibitors (PPIs). They may need to be given this for a
few weeks or months, based on your paediatrician’s medical opinion, or you
may be referred to a paediatric gastroenterologist.
Occasionally, children who wheeze and have asthma-like symptoms may
actually have GERD. This is because when the milk comes up it irritates the
airway, leading to wheezing symptoms and a cough. The baby may get better
with anti-reflux medicines.
Vomiting

Vomiting is a pretty common symptom in babies but there may be certain
situations where you need to be more vigilant:
Vomiting Bile
Bile is a greenish yellow–coloured liquid that is secreted by the liver into the
intestine. So, if your newborn baby vomits yellow bile and if it is associated with
distension or the swelling of the tummy, it could indicate a blockage in the
intestine. You will need to call your doctor or take the baby to the hospital
ASAP.
Vomiting Blood (Coffee Grounds)
Sometimes newborn babies vomit, and the spit-up milk may have some specks
of blood that appears like coffee grounds. This may have come from the
mother’s cracked nipples during the initial few days when breastfeeding was
being established. If you see blood in the vomit after this period, then you need

ffee grounds. This may have come from the
mother’s cracked nipples during the initial few days when breastfeeding was
being established. If you see blood in the vomit after this period, then you need
to speak to your doctor. It may come from bleeding of the lower end of the
oesophagus or stomach. This blood is then partially digested by the stomach
acid.
Vomiting and Fever
Vomiting can be associated with fever and the baby may be a little irritable.
This may be one of the early signs of an infection in the brain, called
meningitis. The fontanelle (the soft spot near the centre of the top of the head)
may appear to bulge because of this infection. You need to call your doctor
immediately.
Vomiting Due to Gastroenteritis

ingitis. The fontanelle (the soft spot near the centre of the top of the head)
may appear to bulge because of this infection. You need to call your doctor
immediately.
Vomiting Due to Gastroenteritis
Repeated vomiting could be because of a stomach infection or gastroenteritis.
Your doctor may ask you to give the baby more fluids and may also prescribe
anti-vomiting medicine. If it doesn’t seem to settle, or your baby doesn’t pass
enough urine, then you will need to call your doctor to check for dehydration.

Vomiting on Waking Up
This is an incredibly rare condition where the baby vomits only in the morning
after she wakes up. This is because of raised pressure in the brain and you
need to rule out a brain tumour.
Vomiting and Dehydration
Q What do I feed my child when he is vomiting, and what are the
signs of dehydration?
It is common sense not to keep feeding your child when he is vomiting
because he is likely to throw up all over you. Wait for 30–45 minutes and
then you can offer her your breastfeed or oral rehydration solution (ORS).
You can start with just a spoonful.
If the baby manages to keep it down, you can give her another spoonful and
build it up slowly. Your doctor may also prescribe an anti-vomiting
medicine. Your baby may be thirsty, but don’t allow her to drink the whole

s to keep it down, you can give her another spoonful and
build it up slowly. Your doctor may also prescribe an anti-vomiting
medicine. Your baby may be thirsty, but don’t allow her to drink the whole
glass in one go because it will all come straight out. If even after this your
baby is vomiting, you may need to call your doctor or take your child to the
hospital as he may need an intravenous drip to prevent dehydration.
Preparing Oral Rehydrating Solutions (ORS) at
home
Ingredients
6 level teaspoons of sugar
½ level teaspoon of salt
1 litre of cooled boiled water

Method
Mix it all. If the child is reluctant to drink, you can replace ½ cup of water with ½ cup of orange
juice or apple juice.
Signs of Dehydration:
The baby is lethargic.
Her lips and skin appear dry.
Her fontanelle is sunken.
She may have tearless crying.
She may be passing less urine.
You need to keep an eye on the number of wet diapers because that will
indicate the state of her hydration.
Burping
Burping is the result of swallowed air during feeds. As babies get older, they
burp far more easily. Don’t spend 20 minutes trying to burp your baby because
either he is going to burp or he isn’t within the first few minutes. There are three
ways that you can try and burp your baby (see p. 26):
You can hold the baby upright on your shoulder and gently rub upwards
on your baby’s back.

he isn’t within the first few minutes. There are three
ways that you can try and burp your baby (see p. 26):
You can hold the baby upright on your shoulder and gently rub upwards
on your baby’s back.
Sit the baby on your lap with you holding his chin so that the baby’s chin
rests on your hand. With your hand supporting the front of your baby’s
chest, rub the baby’s back upwards.
The third way is to put the baby on your lap face down and again rub the
back upwards. The operative word is ‘rubbing’ and not thumping, which
is likely to make the poor baby vomit.
Hiccups

Hiccups are harmless and you really don’t need to do anything about it. They
usually stop on their own. If the baby is hiccupping excessively, you need to
rule out GERD.
Gas
It is normal for babies to pass gas all day since they suck in a lot of air when
they are feeding or crying. Most parents believe that gas is the reason why
babies are unhappy and crying. This is far from the truth. Gas is rarely the
reason for a colicky baby. But if you really feel that you want to give her some
medicine, you can use any anti-colic medication or a probiotic, prescribed by
your doctor, for relief.
It’s best not to give your baby any herbal powders or home-made
preparations without checking with your doctor. Some of these may have

edication or a probiotic, prescribed by
your doctor, for relief.
It’s best not to give your baby any herbal powders or home-made
preparations without checking with your doctor. Some of these may have
ingredients that aren’t suitable for babies, such as honey, janam ghutti, carom
seed (ajwain) powder.
Urine and UTIs
Babies pass little urine in the initial 24–48 hours of their life. This gradually
increases as their milk intake increases.
Q How can I tell if my baby is passing enough urine if he is in
diapers all the time?
You can place a tissue in the diaper and gauge when the baby passes urine.
A diaper that contains urine will feel heavier than a dry diaper. Your doctor
may ask you to keep an eye on the urine output, especially during the early
days of breastfeeding.

the baby passes urine.
A diaper that contains urine will feel heavier than a dry diaper. Your doctor
may ask you to keep an eye on the urine output, especially during the early
days of breastfeeding.
Also, when your baby has been vomiting, it is important to know that your
baby is passing enough urine to see that he is not dehydrated.

QIt looks like my newborn has blood in his pee. Help!
In the newborn period, you may find uric acid crystals in the diaper. These
are more pinkish orange in colour. This is because the baby is mildly
dehydrated and the urine is more concentrated. You don’t need to worry
about it, other than feeding the baby more. But you must mention it to your
baby’s doctor.
In an older child, if there is blood in the urine, you need to speak to your
doctor, as this maybe because of an infection.
If it is a baby girl who has blood in the diaper, then it’s not from the urine but
because of vaginal bleeding or a pseudo period. This is normal, and due to
the hormones passed on from the mother. It will settle in a few days.
QWhat is UTI? Are girls more prone?

from the urine but
because of vaginal bleeding or a pseudo period. This is normal, and due to
the hormones passed on from the mother. It will settle in a few days.
QWhat is UTI? Are girls more prone?
In a UTI (urinary tract infection), there may be foul-smelling urine or an
unexplained fever in children under 6 months. Older babies may complain of
a burning sensation or frequent urination.
The frequency of UTIs is equal in both girls and boys under a year old. For
newborn girls, it is important that you change a potty-filled diaper as soon as
possible. When you clean them, they have to be wiped front to back, i.e.,
from the vagina to the anus. This prevents the germs getting transferred from
the anus to the vaginal area.

diaper as soon as
possible. When you clean them, they have to be wiped front to back, i.e.,
from the vagina to the anus. This prevents the germs getting transferred from
the anus to the vaginal area.
In babies under 3 months, an untreated bladder infection can quickly affect
the kidney, which is a far more serious problem.
Vesicoureteral Reflux (VUR)
Kidney infections may be caused when urine flows from the bladder into the
kidney. This is rare, and is called VUR. There are 5 grades of reflux, where

grade 1 is minimal and grade 5 is severe. Children tend to get recurrent urinary
tract infections, especially when it is grade 4 or 5. They may outgrow a condition
that ranges from grade 1 to grade 3. However, that’s unlikely to happen for those
who are in the grade range of 4 and 5.
We run a test called micturating cystourethrogram (MCUG) which helps
grade the reflux and then the second test, called dimercapto succinic acid
(DMSA) scan, is done to check for any renal scarring. Children who are seen to
have the reflux are put on nightly antibiotics until they outgrow the reflux.

into three categories:
All children under 2 months of age who have a febrile UTI.
